skill.name: planning-with-trello
skill.description: Plan features, sprint work, and Trello synchronization with a simple board structure and repeatable CLI workflow.
skill.summary: Guides Trello-aware planning with board lists, labels, task sizing, and sync commands.
skill.author: BPSAI

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged Markdown backticks as shell execution and ordinary words as weak cryptography. Review found no prompt injection, obfuscation, malware behavior, network exfiltration, or real cryptographic code. The skill is low risk because it documents legitimate external CLI commands that can change local planning data or sync to Trello when executed.

low_findings.title: Documented External CLI Commands
low_findings.description: The skill includes bash examples for bpsai-pair planning and Trello sync commands. This is legitimate for the skill purpose, but users should understand that running the commands may create plans or update Trello boards.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file clearly contains bash command examples for an external CLI. The commands match the skill purpose and show no evidence of command injection or malicious arguments.

low_findings.title: False Positive Markdown Backtick Detection
low_findings.description: Static analysis treated Markdown code fences and inline backticks as Ruby or shell backtick execution. These are documentation formatting markers, not executable code in the skill.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Lines 20-24 are a fenced Markdown card title example and inline documentation. No Ruby code or runtime execution context exists in the file.

low_findings.title: False Positive Weak Cryptography Detection
low_findings.description: Static analysis flagged weak cryptography on lines that contain plain description and table header text. No cryptographic algorithm, hashing function, or encryption operation appears at these locations.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Line 3 is metadata description text and line 28 is an effort-label table header. There is no evidence of DES, MD5, SHA1, RC4, or related cryptographic usage.

dangerous_patterns.title: External Command Examples
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill documents bpsai-pair commands for plan creation, status checks, task listing, and Trello synchronization. These are not malicious, but they are operational commands that can affect project planning state.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The commands are explicit bash examples. They are expected for the Trello planning workflow and do not include suspicious endpoints, shell metacharacters, or secret access.

content.user_title: Plan Trello Workflows with Structured Boards
content.value_statement: Teams often lose planning clarity when tasks, labels, and sprint lists are inconsistent. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code a Trello-focused workflow for planning, sizing, and syncing work.
actual_capabilities: Defines a seven-list Trello board structure for backlog, planned work, active work, review, done work, issues, and notes.
Provides a card title format that groups tasks by stack or work area.
Maps effort labels to small, medium, and large work sizes.
Defines stack labels for frontend, backend, worker, bug, and AI or ML work.
Shows bpsai-pair commands for creating plans, syncing plans to Trello, checking status, and listing tasks.
Encourages 1-2 day task units with acceptance criteria before Trello sync.
limitations: Requires the bpsai-pair CLI and a configured Trello connection before sync commands can work.
Does not include Trello authentication setup or API implementation details.
Uses an opinionated board structure with a maximum of seven lists.
Refers task execution details to a separate managing-task-lifecycle skill.

use_cases.title: Prepare a Feature Sprint
use_cases.description: Break a feature plan into sized tasks and move selected work into a Trello Planned or Ready list.

use_cases.title: Standardize Project Boards
use_cases.description: Create a consistent Trello board layout with shared list names, card titles, and label meanings.

use_cases.title: Sync AI Planning Outputs
use_cases.description: Turn an AI-generated implementation plan into Trello cards through the documented bpsai-pair workflow.
use_cases.target_user: Developer using Claude Code or Codex

prompt_templates.title: Create a Trello Board Plan
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to design a Trello board for my project. Include the recommended lists, label set, and card title rules.

prompt_templates.title: Break Down a Feature
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to turn this feature into 1-2 day Trello tasks with effort labels and acceptance criteria: [describe feature].

prompt_templates.title: Prepare a Sync Preview
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to prepare a dry-run sync plan for plan [plan id]. Show which cards should go to Planned or Ready.

prompt_templates.title: Audit Sprint Board Health
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to review our Trello workflow for planning gaps. Compare our lists, labels, task sizes, and sync process against the skill guidance.

output_examples.input: Plan a new settings page feature for Trello.
output: Recommended card: [Frontend] Build settings page shell.
Effort: M, because it fits a half to full day.
Target list: Planned/Ready after acceptance criteria are confirmed.

output_examples.input: Prepare a sync review for a backend API plan.
output: Review the plan with a dry run before updating Trello.
Use Backend labels for API tasks and Bug/Issue labels for defects.
Move only selected sprint work into Planned/Ready.

output_examples.input: Standardize labels for a mixed product team.
output: Use Frontend, Backend, Worker, Bug/Issue, and AI/ML stack labels.
Use S, M, and L effort labels for planning confidence.
Keep operational decisions in Notes/Ops Log.

best_practices: Run a dry-run sync before updating Trello boards.
Keep tasks small enough to finish within one or two days.
Use consistent stack and effort labels across all planning cards.
anti_patterns: Do not sync vague feature ideas before acceptance criteria are written.
Do not create more than the recommended seven Trello lists.
Do not mix bugs, notes, and active delivery work in the same list.

faq.question: Does this skill connect to Trello by itself?
faq.answer: No. It documents a planning workflow and bpsai-pair commands. Trello access must already be configured.

faq.question: Can I change the list names?
faq.answer: Yes, but the skill is designed around seven clear workflow lists. Keep the same intent if names change.

faq.question: Why should I run a dry run first?
faq.answer: A dry run lets you review planned board changes before any Trello cards are created or moved.

faq.question: What task size should I use for complex work?
faq.answer: Use L for work that spans multiple days. Split it if the task cannot be reviewed clearly.

faq.question: Does this replace sprint planning?
faq.answer: No. It supports sprint planning by making Trello structure, labels, and sync steps more consistent.

faq.question: Which AI tools can use this skill?
faq.answer: The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.
